docs.title: Welcome to Resourceful Sheep!
docs.text:
Resourceful Sheep is a fully customizable Minecraft mod that lets you raise sheep capable of producing different resources, from simple Cobblestone to the mighty Nether Star!

Whether you are a player looking to automate your resource gathering, or a Modpack creator wanting to design complex crossbreeding trees, this wiki contains everything you need to know.

docs.title: What does it do ?
docs.text:
• Resource Production: Each sheep is linked to a specific resource and can be sheared.
  • Tier System: Breed sheep to higher tiers to increase their production output.
  • Crossbreeding: Extract DNA from mobs using Syringes and combine them in the DNA Splicer to create artificial mutations.
  • Highly Configurable: Everything is data-driven. Change drops, feeding items, mutations, and spawning rules using simple JSON files.
  • JEI Integration: Full support for Just Enough Items to view drops, feeding habits, and DNA crossbreeding recipes directly in-game.

docs.title: Where to go next ?
docs.text:
Choose a section below or from the navigation bar to get started:

  • 📖 Getting Started: Learn the basics of finding, feeding, and breeding your first resource sheep.
  • 🧬 Genetics & Machines: Master the art of extracting DNA and using the Sequencer and Splicer.
  • ⚙️ Configuration JSON: The ultimate guide for Modpack makers to customize every aspect of the mod.
  • 📥 Download & Installation: Get the latest version of the mod for your Minecraft client or server.

Found a bug or want to suggest a feature? Head over to our GitHub Issues page.

docs.title: ⚙️ Configuration JSON
docs.text:
Resourceful Sheep is fully customizable. The mod is entirely data-driven, meaning you can create your own sheep types, define mutations, and control where they spawn using simple JSON files.

Don't worry if it looks complex at first, upon first launch, the mod generates plenty of default configuration files that serve as perfect examples to help you understand how the structure works!

File Location

All config files are located in config/resourceful_sheep/ inside your Minecraft instance. They are loaded at game start. Every change needs a full restart of the game /reload is not enough.

docs.title: 1. Sheep Types
docs.text: This is the main configuration file. Each file defines a sheep type and creates variants based on Tiers.

docs.title: 1.1 Global Settings applied to all tiers
docs.text:
• SheepName: UNIQUE resource ID (Example: "cobblestone", "refined_glowstone").
  • EggColorBackground: Hex color for the spawn egg background (Example: "#88771f").
  • EggColorSpotsNTitle: Hex color for the egg spots and the item name hover text.
  • FireImmune: true or false. If true, the sheep cannot be hurt by fire or lava.
  • ImmuneEffects: A list of potion effects that cannot affect the sheep (Example: ["minecraft:poison", "minecraft:wither"]).
  • FoodItems: A list of items used to tempt or breed the sheep.
    • You can use item IDs: "minecraft:carrot"
    • You can use Tags (start with #): "#minecraft:leaves"
  • EtableBocksMap: A list of rules defining what the sheep eats and what the block becomes.
    • Format: "Block_To_Eat": "Block_Result"
    • Tags: You can use tags (only for the input block), example: "#c:cobblestone": "minecraft:air".
    • Destruction: Use "minecraft:air" as the result to destroy the block completely, like eating short grass.

docs.title: 1.2 Tiers & Drops Settings
docs.text:
In the SheepTier list, you define the progression. Each tier creates a unique sheep variant (Example: andesite_tier_1).

  • Tier: An integer defining the tier level.
  • DroppedItems: A list of items dropped when sheared. You can have multiple different items per tier.
    • ItemId: The item ID (e.g., "minecraft:diamond"). This fully supports Tags (e.g., "#forge:ingots/iron").
    • MinDrops / MaxDrops: The quantity range.

docs.title: 1.3 Sheep Type JSON file Example
1.4 Texture Generation
docs.text:
Textures are generated automatically based on the drops. The mod analyzes the colors of the exact items and blocks defined in the variant's DroppedItems list to create a weighted color palette.

Wool color coverage increases by 10% per tier. Maximum coverage is 60%. If a sheep type has more than 6 tiers, the coverage scales proportionally but never exceeds 60%.

docs.title: 2.1 Settings
docs.text:
These files define breeding recipes for new sheep when using classic breeding (feeding two sheep).

  • MomId: ID of the first parent (Example: "iron_tier_1").
  • DadId: ID of the second parent.
  • ChildId: ID of the new sheep.
  • Chance: Integer (1 to 100) representing the chance of success.

Probability Rule

If you define multiple possible mutations for the same parents, their total chance must be ≤ 100. Otherwise, some mutations will be ignored. If no mutation happens or it fails, there is a 50/50 chance to get either parent instead.

docs.title: 2.2 Mutation JSON file Example
docs.text:
steel_tier_2.json
{
"MomId": "steel_tier_1",
"DadId": "steel_tier_1",
"ChildId": "steel_tier_2",
"Chance": 30
}

docs.title: 3.2 Settings
docs.text:
You can create custom DNA crossbreeding recipes for the DNA Splicer machine.

  • MomId: The ID of the first DNA sequence (Example: "resourceful_sheep:gold_tier_1"). Note: You must include the namespace here.
  • DadId: The ID of the second DNA sequence.
  • ChildId: The ID of the resulting sheep if the DNA splicing is successful.
  • Chance: The success rate as a percentage (from 1 to 100).
  • ResultsIfFail: A list of entity IDs that the game will randomly choose from if the crossbreeding fails (Example: ["minecraft:creeper", "minecraft:sheep"]).

Splicing Failure Logic

Unlike natural breeding (Mutations), the parent sheep are not automatically selected upon failure. If you want the player to potentially get the parent DNA back when a Splicer operation fails, you must explicitly add the parents' IDs to the ResultsIfFail list!

docs.title: 3.2 DNA Crossbreeding JSON file Example
docs.text:
wither_tier_1.json
{
"MomId": "minecraft:wither",
"DadId": "minecraft:sheep",
"ChildId": "resourceful_sheep:wither_tier_1",
"ResultsIfFail": [
    "minecraft:sheep",
    "minecraft:wither_skeleton"
],
"Chance": 15
}

docs.title: 4.1 Settings
docs.text:
Here you define how sheep appear in the world naturally.

  • sheepId: Sheep ID (Example: "netherrack_tier_1").
  • minCount / maxCount: Size of the sheep group.
  • densityRadius / maxNearby: Control how often sheep spawn. Roughly maxNearby sheep every densityRadius blocks.
  • requireSeeSky: If set to true, the sheep will only spawn if there are no solid blocks above it (Use true for Overworld surface, false for caves/Nether).
  • Biomes: List of biomes where the sheep can spawn (Example: ["minecraft:plains"]). Tags are supported (e.g., "#minecraft:is_forest"). If left empty, they spawn wherever vanilla sheep spawn.

Experimental Settings Warning

When creating a new world, Minecraft may show a warning about experimental features. This happens because the mod injects a dynamic datapack at runtime to handle sheep spawning cleanly without overwriting other mods. It is 100% safe to ignore and proceed.

If this warning really bothers you or if you are building a modpack and want to hide it from your players, you can completely disable the mod's natural spawning system. To do this, delete all the spawning JSON files and create a single empty JSON file in the folder to prevent the default configs from regenerating.

However, if you do this, the mod offers no alternative way to naturally spawn sheep. You will have to provide another method in your modpack such as custom crafting recipes, custom structures, or using other mods for players to get their very first sheep!

docs.title: 4.2 Spawning JSON file Example
docs.text:
gold_tier_1.json
{
  "sheepId": "gold_tier_1",
  "minCount": 1,
  "maxCount": 1,
  "densityRadius": 1024,
  "maxNearby": 1,
  "Biomes": [
    "minecraft:wooded_badlands"
  ],
  "RequireSeeSky": true
}

docs.title: 5. Helpful Tips
docs.text: • The mod checks your JSON files on startup. If you make a typo in a biome, item, or effect ID, the mod will print a helpful warning in your console logs to help you fix it!

docs.title: 📥 Download & Installation
docs.text: You can download the latest versions of Resourceful Sheep from our official pages. We actively maintain and update the mod on both platforms!

docs.title: Official Links
docs.text: Click on the badges below to go directly to the project pages:

docs.title: Installation Guide
docs.text:
1. Download the mod file .jar matching your Minecraft version from one of the links above.
  2. Make sure you have the correct Mod Loader installed, the mod uses NeoForge only.
  3. Drop the downloaded .jar file into your Minecraft mods folder.
  4. Launch the game and play!

docs.title: 🧬 Genetics & Machines
docs.text: The Genetics system allows you to artificially extract DNA and combine it to create powerful new sheep variations.

docs.title: 💉 Syringes & DNA Extraction
docs.text:
To begin your genetic experiments, you need a Syringe. Right-click on a mob with an empty syringe to extract its DNA. However, not all syringes are strong enough to pierce every mob's skin! You will need to upgrade your syringe to extract DNA from more dangerous creatures.

Here is exactly what each Syringe tier can target:

  • Iron Syringe: Works on peaceful and basic creatures. This includes animals (like Sheep, Cows, and Pigs), Villagers, Water Animals (Fish, Squids), Bats, Allays, and Golems. Cannot be used on hostile monsters or Shulkers.
  • Diamond Syringe: Works on everything the Iron Syringe does, plus hostile creatures! This allows you to extract DNA from Monsters (Zombies, Creepers, Skeletons), Slimes, and Flying Mobs (Ghasts, Phantoms). Cannot be used on the Wither or the Warden.
  • Netherite Syringe: The ultimate extraction tool. It works on almost every mob in the game, allowing you to finally extract DNA from the terrifying Warden and Bosses like the Wither or the Ender Dragon!

docs.title: 💉 Syringe Upgrades
docs.text: You start with an Iron Syringe, but it can be upgraded to Diamond and Netherite using the Smithing Table. To do this, you need a specific Smithing Template. You can find a diamond smithing template in two ways: 1. Loot: Found randomly in chests inside Shepherd village houses. 2. Trading: A Level 3 Shepherd Villager has about a 26% chance to sell it to you for Emeralds.

docs.title: 🔬 The DNA Sequencer
docs.text:
Once you have a filled Syringe, you need to analyze it. Place the filled Syringe into the DNA Sequencer. Provide the machine with FE, and it will begin analyzing the DNA.

Once finished, the sequence is saved in the machine's internal memory.

docs.title: 🧬 The DNA Splicer
docs.text:
The DNA Splicer is where the magic happens. This machine takes two different DNA sequences and attempts to splice them together to create a new mutation. With the default config this machine needs to be adjacent to a DNA Sequencer.

Check the Crossbreeding Category in JEI to see which DNA combinations produce which results!

docs.title: 🥚 Suspicious Spawn Egg
docs.text:
When the DNA Splicer finishes its operation, it will output a Suspicious Spawn Egg.

However, you won't know if the mutation was a success or a failure just by looking at it! Simply place this egg on the ground to hatch it and reveal your creation. Will it be a brand new, powerful genetic mutation, or a failed experiment?

docs.title: 📖 Getting Started
docs.text: Welcome to the world of Resourceful Sheep! If you are new to the mod, this guide will help you understand the core mechanics and get your first automated resource farm running.

docs.title: 1. Finding Your First Sheep
docs.text: Resourceful Sheep spawn naturally in the world, just like vanilla sheep. However, their spawning locations depend on their type! For example, Sand or Cobblestone sheep might be found in Plains or Forests, while Netherrack sheep will only be found in the Nether.

docs.title: 2. Feeding & Tempting
docs.text:
Just like normal sheep, Resourceful Sheep need to eat grass to regrow their wool after being sheared. However, different types of sheep might have specific diets! Some might eat grass, while other sheep eat nylium.

To tempt them into a pen or breed them, you will need specific items. While vanilla sheep use Wheat, a Netherrack Sheep might require nether wart!

JEI is your best friend

Use the Just Enough Items (JEI) mod! We have added custom categories that show you exactly what every sheep eats, what items tempt them, and what they drop.

docs.title: 3. Shearing for Resources
docs.text: Once a sheep's wool is fully grown, right-click it with vanilla Shears. In addition to regular wool, the sheep will also drop its associated resources as Iron Nuggets, Redstone dust, or Diamonds!

docs.title: 4. Natural Breeding (Mutations)
docs.text:
Want a better sheep ? You can crossbreed them! By feeding two different compatible sheep their favorite food, they will breed. If they are compatible, there is a chance they will produce a mutated offspring of a higher tier or a completely different resource type!

Mutation Failure

Mutations are not guaranteed. If a mutation fails, the baby sheep has a 50/50 chance to simply be the exact same type as one of its parents. Check JEI for exact mutation chances!
